issues.description: The sign-chart workflow is presented as universal ('find the critical points, plot them on a number line, test the sign of f prime in each interval') but only mentions critical points as the dividers. A learner will generalize this to rational/discontinuous functions and forget to also mark points where f is undefined or discontinuous (vertical asymptotes, domain breaks), producing wrong increasing/decreasing intervals.
issues.fix: Add a half-sentence: 'and mark any points where f itself is undefined or discontinuous — those also break the number line, even though they aren't critical points.'

issues.description: Every worked example (x^3, x^3-3x) is a smooth polynomial whose critical points come only from f'=0. The 'or where f prime does not exist' half of the critical-point definition is never demonstrated, so a reasonable learner can walk away believing critical points are always solutions of f'(x)=0 and miss corner/cusp extrema (e.g. f(x)=|x| or x^(2/3)).
issues.fix: Include one quick non-differentiable example (e.g. f(x)=x^(2/3) has a minimum at x=0 where f' does not exist) so the 'f prime undefined' case is concretely modeled, not just stated.

issues.description: Critical point is defined as 'an interior point c where f prime equals zero or does not exist.' The 'interior' qualifier is technically used to keep endpoints out (handled later by the Candidates Test), but it is dropped silently and never explained. A careful student may conflate this with the more common textbook definition (a point in the domain of f) and be confused about why endpoints are excluded here but re-added in section 12.
issues.fix: One clause linking the two: 'we say interior because the closed-interval endpoints get handled separately in the Candidates Test, not because endpoints can never be extrema.'

issues.description: Inflection point is correctly tied to a sign change of f'' plus f defined there, but no example is given where f''=0 yet there is NO inflection (e.g. x^4 at 0). Since section 14 uses x^4 to show f''=0 is not a minimum-disqualifier, a learner could cross-wire and assume x^4 has an inflection at 0 (it does not). The 'f''=0 alone is not enough' point is asserted but never shown with the negative example.
issues.fix: Reuse x^4: 'For example x to the fourth has f double prime equal zero at the origin, but f double prime stays positive on both sides, so there is no sign change and no inflection point there.'

notes: Script pre-empts the headline traps (critical point != extremum via x^3, second-derivative-test f''=0 inconclusive via x^3 vs x^4, MVT/EVT hypotheses, endpoints in Candidates Test) correctly; remaining gaps are all about under-modeling the non-differentiable and discontinuous cases.
